wizard.step1Description: Define basic rule information and confidence requirements

step2.enterPattern: Enter text or regex pattern or use regex generator
step2.tooltipLogicGroup: A logic group contains one or more patterns. Use 'Match any (OR)' when any pattern can identify the document. Use 'Match all (AND)' when all patterns must be present. Check 'Mandatory' to make this group required for classification - if mandatory groups don't match, the rule fails regardless of POCO score.
step2.tooltipPattern: Enter a simple text string to search for, or use the Pattern Helper to build flexible patterns, or write your own regex for advanced matching.

wizard.step2Description: Define patterns that identify this document type in OCR text
wizard.step3Description: Define patterns that identify this document type by filename
wizard.ocrExampleText: Example: If all {count} pattern{plural} match, OCR Score = ({count}/{count}) × 100 = 100%. Max OCR weight = {weight} points.
wizard.ocrScoreChangedWarning: OCR Score requirement changed from default (75%).
wizard.filenameExampleText: Example: With {count} pattern{plural} defined, max filename weight = {count} × {count} × {multiplier} = {weight} points.
wizard.verificationDescription: Select which Paperless placeholders must be verified and configure verification multiplier.
wizard.verificationOptionalNote: This step is optional. If you don't enable any fields, it will remain disabled and you can proceed to the next step.
wizard.noPlaceholdersEnabled: No placeholders enabled. Enable placeholders in Settings > Step 5.
wizard.step3Of6Title: Step 3 of 6: Defining Static Classification Data
wizard.staticDataDescription: Configure fixed classification data that applies to all documents matching this rule.
wizard.staticMetadataDescription: Configure fixed information that applies to all documents matching this rule. This is constant data assigned once a document is recognised.
wizard.documentClassificationsDescription: Configure document classification data extracted from OCR
wizard.staticClassificationDataInfoText: This is constant data you want to assign to a document once it's recognised. Greyed out fields are automatically populated by the system.
wizard.staticMetadataInfoText: This is constant data you want to assign to a document once it's recognised. For example, every statement of a certain type will always have the same correspondent assigned.
wizard.reviewBeforeSaving: Review your rule configuration before saving

help.scenarioIntro: Example scenario: You have an electricity bill from your energy provider and want PocoClass to automatically recognise all future electricity bills.

step1.whatText: This is where you give your rule a name and decide how strict PocoClass should be when matching documents. Think of it as labelling a folder and setting how confident the system must be before filing something into it.
step1.ruleNameWhy: A clear, descriptive name you'll recognise later
step1.ruleIdWhy: Generated automatically from the name — used internally
step1.description: Identifies monthly electricity bills from any energy provider
step1.descriptionWhy: Optional but helpful when you have many rules
step1.pocoThresholdWhy: Default — means 75% of all evidence must match
step1.ocrThresholdWhy: Default — means 75% of OCR text patterns must be found
step1.tip1: Keep the default thresholds (75%) unless you have a reason to change them. Lower values may cause false matches, higher values may miss valid documents.
step1.tip2: The rule name should describe the document type, not a specific document. For example, use "Electricity Bill" rather than "January 2024 Electricity Bill".

step2.whatText: This is the most important step. Here you define which words or phrases PocoClass should look for inside a document's text content (OCR). These patterns are how the system recognises a document type — similar to how you would scan a page and look for key words to identify what kind of document it is.
step2.conceptText: Patterns are organised into logic groups. Each group contains one or more patterns.
step2.conceptOR: Match Any (OR) — The group matches if at least one pattern is found. Use this when different documents use different wording for the same thing.
step2.conceptAND: Match All (AND) — The group matches only if every pattern is found. Use this for patterns that must always appear together.
step2.conceptMandatory: Mandatory — If checked, this group must match or the entire rule fails, regardless of the score. Use sparingly for truly essential identifiers.
step2.exampleIntro: An electricity bill typically contains certain words you won't find in other documents. Here's how you might set up the groups:
step2.group1Why: One of these words should always appear on an electricity bill. Set as mandatory because without it, the document is probably not an electricity bill.
step2.group2Why: Electricity bills mention consumption in specific units. Any one of these confirms it's energy-related.
step2.group3Why: These terms distinguish a bill from other energy-related documents like contracts or informational letters.
step2.tip1: Aim for at least 3 logic groups. The more varied your patterns are, the more reliably PocoClass can distinguish this document from similar ones.
step2.tip2: Use simple text patterns first — you don't need regex for most cases. "kWh" is a perfectly good pattern.
step2.tip3: Open the OCR viewer (eye icon) to see the actual text content of a document. This helps you pick patterns that are really there.
step2.tip4: The search range (lines) lets you limit where patterns are searched. For example, the word "Electricity" usually appears near the top of the bill (lines 0–200), while "Amount Due" could be anywhere.

step3.whatText: Here you define patterns that match against the document's filename — not its content. This is a secondary identification method. Filenames often contain useful clues like the document type or provider name.
step3.filenameExplanation: The filename contains 'electricity_bill' — a simple pattern like 'electricity' or 'bill' would match this.
step3.tip1: Filename patterns are optional but can help distinguish similar documents. They are most useful when your files follow a consistent naming convention.

step4.whatText: This step lets you cross-check extracted data with what Paperless-ngx already knows. For example, if PocoClass extracted a correspondent name, this step can verify it matches an existing correspondent in Paperless. It's an extra layer of confidence.
step4.tip1: This step is optional. If you're just starting out, you can skip it and come back later once your basic rules are working reliably.

step5.whatText: This is where you tell PocoClass what to do once a document is recognised. You assign classification data — like which Correspondent, Document Type, and Tags to apply. You can also extract dynamic data from the document content using anchors.
step5.tip1: Static data (like Correspondent = 'GreenPower Energy') is applied identically to every matching document. Dynamic data (like extracting the invoice date) is pulled from each document individually.

step6.whatText: This final step shows a summary of everything you have configured. Review it carefully before saving. You can go back to any previous step to make changes.

ocr_identifiers_description: Define patterns that identify this document type in OCR text
ocr_identifiers_warning: ⚠️ Warning: You have filled only {filledGroups} of 3 required logic groups. Please add patterns to at least 3 logic groups for reliable classification.
ocr_score_requirement_tooltip: Minimum percentage of OCR patterns that must match for this rule to contribute to classification. 75% means at least 75% of your defined patterns must be found in the document's OCR text.
ocr_weight_multiplier_tooltip: Controls how much weight OCR pattern matches have in the final POCO score. Higher values make OCR matching more important. Default is 3× for strong OCR-based classification.
change_ocr_score_requirement_message: You're changing the OCR Score requirement from 75% (recommended) to {pendingOcrThreshold}%. Are you sure?
change_ocr_weight_multiplier_message: You're changing the OCR weight multiplier from 3× (recommended) to {pendingMultiplier}×. This affects how much OCR patterns influence the final POCO score. Are you sure?

backgroundProcessing.title: Background Processing
backgroundProcessing.subtitle: Configure automatic document classification for newly uploaded documents
backgroundProcessing.enableProcessingDesc: Automatically classify documents when they are uploaded to Paperless-ngx
backgroundProcessing.debounceDesc: Wait time after detecting new documents before processing (prevents multiple runs for batch uploads). Range: 5-300 seconds.
backgroundProcessing.tagConfigurationDesc: PocoClass uses these fixed tags for document processing and classification
backgroundProcessing.tagNewDesc: Discovery tag - Documents with this tag will be automatically processed by background processing
backgroundProcessing.tagPocoPlusDesc: Success tag - Added when a document matches a rule and passes both POCO and OCR thresholds
backgroundProcessing.tagPocoMinusDesc: Failure tag - Added when a document is processed but doesn't match any rule or fails thresholds
backgroundProcessing.removeNewTagDesc: When enabled, PocoClass will automatically remove the NEW tag from documents after processing them. This helps keep your inbox clean by removing the trigger tag once a document has been classified.
backgroundProcessing.retentionPolicyDesc: Control how long processing history is kept in the database
backgroundProcessing.daysToKeepDesc: Processing runs older than this will be automatically deleted (default: 365 days / 1 year)

systemToken.title: System API Token
systemToken.description: Generate a permanent API token for automated triggering of background processing via scripts or external systems.
systemToken.confirmRegenerate: This will invalidate the existing token. Continue?
systemToken.confirmRevoke: Revoke the system token? Scripts using it will stop working.
systemToken.generatedDesc: Copy and save this token now - it cannot be retrieved later.
systemToken.revokedDesc: The system token has been revoked. Scripts using it will no longer work.
systemToken.saveWarning: Save this token now - it will not be shown again!

systemToken.adminOnly: Only administrators can manage the system API token
systemToken.scriptSetupDesc: For automatic processing, you must configure a post-consumption script in Paperless-ngx. Copy pococlass_trigger.sh to your scripts folder and set the environment variable:
systemToken.copyWarning: Make sure to copy this token - it will not be shown again!

settings.ensureAllRequired: Ensure all required items are configured

settings.pocoMinus: Failure tag - Added when a document is processed but doesn't match any rule or fails thresholds

settings.pocoOcrTransparencyDesc: Optional field to store the OCR pattern matching transparency score. This score shows how well the document matches the OCR patterns (0-100%). Helps verify confidence in OCR-based classification.

backgroundProcess.triggerNowDesc: Automatically discovers and processes all documents tagged "NEW" (operates independently of filters below).
backgroundProcess.testRulesAgainst: Test rules against filtered documents. Select documents below using the filter bar, then choose your testing mode.

backgroundProcess.dryRunNote: This is a test run. No changes will be made to Paperless.
backgroundProcess.autoPauseWarningTitle: Automatic processing pauses while you are logged in

validation.lowercaseOnly: Only lowercase letters, numbers, and underscores allowed
validation.ruleNameMinLength: Name must be at least 3 characters
validation.ruleIdFormat: Only lowercase letters, numbers, and underscores

patterns.enterPattern: Enter text or regex pattern or use regex generator

placeholders.description: Describe what documents this rule identifies...
placeholders.enterPattern: Enter text or regex pattern or use regex generator
placeholders.addTag: Add a tag and press Enter

tooltips.basicInfoHelp: Basic information helps identify your rule and control classification behavior. The POCO Score requirement determines how confident the system must be before classifying a document. For more details, see the Guide in the sidebar.
tooltips.pocoScoreHelp: The minimum overall confidence score required for a document to be classified by this rule. The POCO score combines OCR content matching, filename patterns, and metadata verification. 75% is recommended for balanced accuracy.
tooltips.pocoScoreDefault: Default: 75% is recommended for balanced accuracy between catching valid documents and avoiding false positives.
tooltips.ocrThresholdHelp: The minimum percentage of OCR patterns that must match for a document to be classified. This ensures the document content actually matches what you're looking for.
tooltips.ocrThresholdDefault: Default: 75% is recommended to ensure reliable OCR matching.
tooltips.filenameHelp: Filename patterns help identify documents by their file names. Use the Pattern Helper to create flexible patterns that handle variations in naming conventions.
tooltips.filenamePatternHelp: Enter a simple text string to search for in filenames, or use the Pattern Helper to build flexible patterns, or write your own regex for advanced matching.
tooltips.filenameMultiplierHelp: Controls how much influence filename patterns have in the final POCO score. Higher values mean filenames are more important.
tooltips.filenameMultiplierDefault: Default: 1× multiplier is recommended because filenames are less reliable than OCR content for classification.
tooltips.verificationHelp: Verify that extracted data matches existing Paperless metadata. This adds confidence to the classification by checking if the document's placeholders align with what was extracted from OCR.
tooltips.dynamicDataHelp: Dynamic data extraction allows you to pull specific information from documents using anchor points. Define patterns before and after the data you want to extract.
tooltips.extractionRuleHelp: Each extraction rule identifies and extracts specific data from the document using before and after anchor patterns.
tooltips.beforeAnchorHelp: Text or pattern that appears before the data you want to extract. Can be simple text or regex.
tooltips.extractTypeHelp: Defines what type of data to extract: simple text, numeric amounts, dates, or multiple lines of text.
tooltips.afterAnchorHelp: Text or pattern that appears after the data you want to extract. Can be simple text or regex.
tooltips.targetTagHelp: Select one or more tags where the extracted data should be stored in Paperless-ngx.

guide.title: PocoClass Guide
guide.subtitle: A beginner-friendly guide to document classification

introduction.title: Introduction
introduction.p1: PocoClass is an intelligent rule-based classification engine designed to enhance Paperless-ngx. While Paperless already provides a powerful search interface and a built-in classifier, it might struggle to recognize complex document types, especially during initial bulk imports or when documents follow complex formats. PocoClass fills this gap by letting you explicitly define how each type of document should be identified through patterns and how it should be classified.
introduction.p2: Its primary objective is to deliver consistent, accurate classification—no matter how many documents you are importing or how varied they may be. Once configured, PocoClass can run fully automatically, quietly keeping your Paperless library structured with minimal effort from you.
introduction.paperlessPopupP1: Paperless-ngx is a digital document management system designed to help you organize, store, and search your paperwork electronically. Instead of keeping physical folders filled with invoices, contracts, letters, or receipts, Paperless-ngx allows you to scan or upload these documents and manage them in one secure, searchable archive.
introduction.paperlessPopupP2: When you upload a document, Paperless automatically processes it using OCR (Optical Character Recognition). OCR extracts the text from scanned images or PDFs and makes that text searchable. This means you can later find a document simply by typing a keyword that appears inside it — even if it was originally a scanned paper document.
introduction.paperlessPopupP3: Documents in Paperless can be organized using structured metadata such as Correspondent (who the document is from), Document Type (what kind of document it is), Tags (flexible labels), and Custom Fields (additional information specific to your needs).
introduction.paperlessPopupP4: Paperless-ngx also supports workflows and automation rules, allowing documents to be classified or tagged automatically based on defined conditions.
introduction.paperlessPopupP5: In short, Paperless-ngx replaces your physical filing cabinet with a powerful, searchable digital archive — helping you reduce paper clutter, improve organization, and retrieve documents instantly when you need them.

howItWorks.title: How PocoClass Works with Paperless
howItWorks.intro: PocoClass works alongside Paperless to automatically organize your documents. Here's how it all fits together:
howItWorks.step1Desc: You scan or upload a document. Paperless extracts the text (OCR) so it can be searched. Paperless automatically notifies PocoClass that one or more new documents are available for analysis.
howItWorks.step2Desc: PocoClass checks the document against your personal defined rules. It looks at the text, filename, and other information, then calculates a confidence score (POCO Score) for each possible match. The scoring system evaluates how well the document fits each rule.
howItWorks.step3Desc: If a match is found, PocoClass automatically applies the classifications you specified in your rule—like tags, correspondents, document type, and other details—directly to the document in Paperless.
howItWorks.step4Desc: Your document now has the right classification and is easy to find. You can also use Paperless workflows to do additional processing based on these classifications, like automatically moving documents to specific folders.
howItWorks.autoProcessingDesc: PocoClass is triggered automatically whenever Paperless finishes consuming documents. It applies your rules without any manual action required, ensuring consistent classification for bulk imports and daily new documents.
howItWorks.whatCanEditTitle: What PocoClass Can Edit in Paperless
howItWorks.whatCanEditIntro: PocoClass updates your documents directly in Paperless once a rule matches. Depending on your rule configuration, PocoClass can set or update the following fields:
howItWorks.correspondentDesc: Who the document is from (e.g., a bank, insurer, or government agency)
howItWorks.documentTypeDesc: The category of the document (e.g., invoice, statement, receipt, contract)
howItWorks.tagsDesc: Labels that help group or filter documents (e.g., "Finance", "Tax", "2024")
howItWorks.customFieldsDesc: Additional information extracted from the document, such as invoice numbers, reference codes, dates, or account numbers
howItWorks.updatesNote: All these updates happen within Paperless itself, meaning your library stays organized, searchable, and consistent.

scoring.title: The Scoring System
scoring.intro: PocoClass uses a confidence-based scoring system to decide whether a document matches a rule. This score determines whether the rule is applied, skipped, or flagged for review.
scoring.twoScoresIntro: To keep the process transparent, PocoClass generates two scores for every document:
scoring.ocrScoreDesc: This score reflects how many OCR identifiers were found in the document text. It shows how well the document content matches your rule.
scoring.ocrScorePoint1: It acts as a gatekeeper: if the OCR score is below the OCR Threshold (default 75%), rule evaluation stops immediately
scoring.ocrScorePoint2: This threshold is configurable per rule in the rule builder
scoring.ocrScoreNote: If a rule requires 75% OCR accuracy and the document only matches 50%, PocoClass stops here and will not continue with filename or Paperless checks.
scoring.pocoScoreIntro: Once OCR passes the minimum threshold, PocoClass evaluates the full document using:
scoring.pocoScoreNote: Each domain contributes to the final score according to its number of identifiers and its trust multiplier. The final POCO Score must meet the POCO Threshold (default 75%) for the rule to be applied.
scoring.calculationIntro: PocoClass evaluates each data source using this formula:
scoring.multipliersIntro: Multipliers control how much each data source contributes to the final score. A higher multiplier means that source carries more weight in determining classification confidence.
scoring.ocrMultiplier: OCR: 3× - OCR text is usually very reliable
scoring.classificationsMultiplier: Classifications: Auto - By default, the classification multiplier neutralizes the number of Paperless classifications, ensuring they can influence confidence without overpowering OCR
scoring.adjustPoint1: Using unreliable OCR? Lower the OCR multiplier
scoring.adjustPoint2: Filenames are super reliable in your organization? Raise the Filename multiplier
scoring.adjustPoint3: Trust Paperless classifications more? Adjust accordingly
scoring.examplesThresholdNote: Understanding the Threshold Percentage: The scores shown here are absolute values. To convert them to the percentages you define in the rule builder (like 75%), divide the score by the maximum possible score for that rule. The maximum possible score is what you'd get if all identifiers matched perfectly.
scoring.example1Result:
Maximum possible: (5 × 3) + (2 × 1) + (4 × 0.25) = 18
Percentage: 14.75 ÷ 18 = 82%
If your threshold is 75% → ✓ Classification applied (82% exceeds 75%)
scoring.example2Result:
Maximum possible: 18 (same as Example 1)
Percentage: 9.5 ÷ 18 = 53%
If your threshold is 75% → ✗ Rule skipped (53% is below 75%)
scoring.example3Title: Example 3 – OCR Failure (Filename & Paperless Can't Save It)
scoring.example3Result:
Maximum possible: 18 (same as Example 1)
Percentage: 6 ÷ 18 = 33%
If your threshold is 75% → ✗ Rule skipped (even perfect filename & Paperless can't compensate for weak OCR)
scoring.ruleOfThumbPoint1: If OCR is strong, filename and Paperless usually confirm it.
scoring.ruleOfThumbPoint2: If OCR is borderline, filename and Paperless decide whether the rule passes or fails.
scoring.ruleOfThumbPoint3: If OCR is weak, the rule never proceeds.
scoring.whereStoredTitle: Where Scores Are Stored in Paperless
scoring.whereStoredIntro: PocoClass writes its evaluation results back into Paperless so you always know what happened:
scoring.whereStoredOCR: POCO OCR Score – OCR-only confidence (optional custom field). Every document gets an OCR score recorded, even if it's 0%
scoring.whereStoredPlus: POCO+ tag – rule matched and classification applied
scoring.whereStoredMinus: POCO- tag – rule evaluated but did not meet the threshold
scoring.documentNotesDesc: A detailed scoring breakdown is also appended to the document's notes in Paperless, providing full transparency for every evaluation.

setup.title: Setup Requirements & Data Synchronization
setup.intro: Before PocoClass can work, you need to set up a few things in your Paperless system. These required fields allow PocoClass to track its work and store important information.
setup.pocoScoreDesc: Stores the confidence score calculated by PocoClass for each document after rule evaluation
setup.pocoOCRDesc: Stores the OCR-specific confidence score component for analysis and troubleshooting
setup.newTagDesc: Applied to documents immediately after Paperless consumes them, marking them as candidates for PocoClass processing. PocoClass uses this to identify new documents requiring rule evaluation
setup.plusTagDesc: Applied to documents where PocoClass's classification exceeds the minimum POCO Score threshold, indicating successful matches
setup.minusTagDesc: Applied to documents where PocoClass's classification falls below the minimum POCO Score threshold, indicating insufficient confidence
setup.managedIn: Custom fields and tag requirements are managed in
setup.syncArchDesc: PocoClass employs a caching strategy to minimize API overhead on your Paperless instance. Rather than querying Paperless on every document evaluation, PocoClass fetches and caches the current state of all tags, correspondents, document types, and custom fields during synchronization. Without caching, each document processed could require hundreds of API calls to resolve all these classification references. This cached state remains valid until explicitly refreshed, significantly reducing API calls while maintaining data consistency.
setup.autoSyncDesc: Synchronization happens automatically without manual intervention. PocoClass monitors for schema changes and re-syncs when you return to the application after switching tabs or leaving the interface. The system evaluates whether re-synchronization is needed and triggers it transparently—keeping your cached state current with minimal overhead.
setup.forceSyncNote: In most cases, automatic synchronization handles all data consistency needs. If you need to force an immediate update, it can be managed through

buildingRules.title: Building Rules: The 6-Step Wizard
buildingRules.intro: A rule tells PocoClass: "If a document contains these patterns, classify it like this."
buildingRules.wizardIntro: The rule builder wizard guides you step by step through defining how a document should be identified and what classifications should be assigned when it matches.
buildingRules.step1Intro: Give your rule a clear identity:
buildingRules.ruleName: Rule Name – A descriptive, human-friendly name (e.g., Bank Statements, Tax Letters)
buildingRules.ruleId: Rule ID – A unique identifier automatically generated. You may customize it for readability
buildingRules.description: Description – Optional notes to remind yourself what the rule does
buildingRules.step1Note: This helps keep your rules organized, especially once you build many of them.
buildingRules.step2Intro: In this step you define the text patterns PocoClass should look for in the OCR text extracted from the document.
buildingRules.matchDates: Dates or short phrases unique to a document format
buildingRules.chooseMatch: Choose how they must match (ALL / ANY / logic groups)
buildingRules.setOCRThreshold: Set the OCR Threshold (default: 75%) – this determines how many patterns must match before PocoClass considers the rule valid
buildingRules.ocrContinueNote: PocoClass only continues to filename and classifications evaluation if the OCR threshold is met. (See the scoring section for details.)
buildingRules.regexIntro: You may use simple text patterns or more advanced regular expressions (regex).
buildingRules.regexOptionalNote: Regex is optional — most rules work fine with simple patterns.
buildingRules.regexBuilderTip: Tip: There is a built-in Regex Builder (available in the pattern editor) to assist in creating complex patterns without writing code manually.
buildingRules.step3Intro: Filename patterns allow PocoClass to use the document's filename as a secondary signal.
buildingRules.filenameAfterOCR: They are evaluated only after OCR passes
buildingRules.filenameMultiplierNote: Their influence is controlled by the Filename Multiplier (default: 1×)
buildingRules.step4Intro: This is where you define how confident PocoClass must be before applying the rule.
buildingRules.ocrThreshold: OCR Threshold – Minimum percentage of OCR patterns that must match
buildingRules.pocoThreshold: POCO Threshold – Minimum final score required for the rule to apply
buildingRules.filenameMultiplierConfig: Filename Multiplier – Trust level for filename matching (default: 1×)
buildingRules.classificationMultiplierConfig: Classification Multiplier – Trust level for Paperless classifications
buildingRules.step4Note: These settings determine how heavily each data source influences the final score. For full details, see The Scoring System.
buildingRules.step5Intro: Here you define what PocoClass should write into Paperless if the rule matches.
buildingRules.staticDesc: Values that always apply when the rule matches:
buildingRules.staticExample: Example: Assign Correspondent → "Bank XYZ", Assign Document Type → "Monthly Statement", Tags → Finance, 2024
buildingRules.dynamicDesc: Extracted directly from the document using anchors.
buildingRules.dynamicExampleIntro: You want to extract an invoice number that appears after "Inv: "
buildingRules.step6Intro: Verification allows PocoClass to confirm that the extracted or assigned classifications aligns with what is already stored in Paperless.
buildingRules.step6Example: Example: Extracted Correspondent → "John Smith", Paperless Correspondent → "John Smith" → Verification passes
buildingRules.step6Note: If verification fails, you can choose to allow the rule anyway or block it from applying. Verification adds an extra safety layer, especially when similar document formats may overlap.

testing.title: Testing Your Rules
testing.intro: Before you let PocoClass automatically classify hundreds of documents, you want to make sure your rule works correctly on real documents.
testing.dryRunQuestion: "What would happen if I ran this rule?"
testing.dryRunPoint1: PocoClass tests your rule against selected documents
testing.dryRunPoint2: Shows you what would happen (classifications, scores)
testing.dryRunPoint3: Does not make any changes to Paperless
testing.dryRunPoint4: Perfect for testing before you're confident
testing.fullRunPoint1: PocoClass tests your rule and actually classifies the documents
testing.fullRunPoint3: Applied tags, correspondents, and classifications to documents
testing.fullRunPoint4: Scores are recorded in the POCO Score custom field
testing.understandingIntro: When you test, PocoClass shows you a report with:
testing.resultOCR: OCR Score: What % of OCR patterns matched?
testing.resultPOCO: POCO Score: What was the final classification score?
testing.resultClassification: Classification: POCO+ (matched) or POCO- (no match) - shows whether the rule matched the document
testing.resultApplied: Classifications Applied: What got assigned (correspondent, tags, etc.) - which identifiers like OCR or filename were successful
testing.scoresOCR: OCR Score – Percentage of OCR patterns that matched
testing.scoresPOCO: POCO Score – Combined confidence from OCR, filename, and Paperless
testing.scoresTrigger: Rule triggers when POCO Score meets your threshold (default 75%)
testing.barChartIntro: Test results include a bar chart showing:
testing.barBlue: Blue bar = OCR Score (how many patterns matched)
testing.barGreen: Green/Red bar = POCO Score (final decision score)
testing.barOrange: Orange line = Your threshold (75% by default)
testing.barNote: If the bars reach the orange line, the rule triggers.

backgroundProcessing.title: Background Processing
backgroundProcessing.intro: Background processing is how PocoClass automatically classifies new documents.
backgroundProcessing.howItWorksIntro: When triggered, PocoClass scans for documents with the NEW tag and applies your classification rules. Your administrator configures how triggering works (automatic via post-consumption script, manual, or scheduled).
backgroundProcessing.step1: Step 1: The Trigger - PocoClass looks for documents with the "NEW" tag
backgroundProcessing.step2: Step 2: Filter Documents - Find documents tagged with "NEW" and NOT already tagged with "POCO+" or "POCO-"
backgroundProcessing.step3: Step 3: Apply Rules - Run all enabled rules against these documents in order
backgroundProcessing.step4: Step 4: Tag & Score - Apply classifications, write POCO Score and POCO OCR, apply POCO+ or POCO- tag, optionally remove NEW tag (if enabled in Settings)
backgroundProcessing.step5: Step 5: Repeat - Continue looking for more documents with the "NEW" tag
backgroundProcessing.tipNewTag: Tip: You can choose whether PocoClass automatically removes the NEW tag after processing. Enable this option to keep your Paperless inbox clean, or leave it disabled if you prefer to manually verify documents before removing the tag yourself.
backgroundProcessing.automaticPoint1: Runs continuously in the background every few minutes
backgroundProcessing.automaticPoint2: Always watching for new documents with "NEW" tag
backgroundProcessing.automaticPoint3: Pauses when you're using the web interface (so it doesn't interfere)
backgroundProcessing.automaticPoint4: Pauses when no documents need processing (saves resources)
backgroundProcessing.triggerPoint1: Runs once when you manually ask it to
backgroundProcessing.triggerPoint2: Useful for testing or processing a specific batch of documents
backgroundProcessing.triggerPoint3: You click "Trigger Background Processing" and it processes once
backgroundProcessing.dryRunPoint1: Same as Trigger Mode, but doesn't make changes
backgroundProcessing.dryRunPoint2: Shows you what would happen without actually classifying
backgroundProcessing.historyIntro: PocoClass keeps track of what it processed:
backgroundProcessing.summaryPoint2: What type of run (Automatic, Manual, Dry Run)
backgroundProcessing.detailPoint4: What classification it got (POCO+ or POCO-)

aboutName.title: About the Name
aboutName.p1: The name PocoClass originates from the earliest script this project was based on, called POCO. POCO stands for Post Consumption and referred to a simple script that was triggered by Paperless through its built-in post-consumption mechanism.
aboutName.p2: That original script performed basic rule-based classification immediately after documents were imported. Over time, this concept evolved into a full document classification system with scoring, verification, and a web-based interface. The name PocoClass was kept to reflect these origins while representing the broader classification capabilities the project now provides.

quickStart.title: Quick Start
quickStart.subtitle: Get up and running with PocoClass

howItWorks.title: How PocoClass Works
howItWorks.description: PocoClass watches for documents tagged with "NEW" in Paperless, matches them against your classification rules, and applies the appropriate tags and metadata. Documents that pass get a POCO+ tag; those that don't get a POCO- tag.

minimumSetup.title: Minimum Setup Requirements
minimumSetup.intro: Before you can use PocoClass, your Paperless system needs these items:
minimumSetup.pocoScore: POCO Score – a custom field to store classification confidence
minimumSetup.pocoOCR: POCO OCR (optional) – a custom field to store OCR transparency score
minimumSetup.newTag: NEW tag – marks documents ready for processing (Paperless must be configured to assign this tag to incoming documents)
minimumSetup.checkSettings: Go to Settings → Data Validation to check if these exist. PocoClass can create missing items for you.

scoring.title: How Scoring Works
scoring.intro: PocoClass uses a confidence-based scoring system to decide if a document matches a rule:
scoring.ocrScore: OCR Score – percentage of text patterns found in the document. Must reach 75% (default) for the rule to continue.
scoring.pocoScore: POCO Score – combines OCR, filename, and Paperless verification into a final confidence score. Must reach 75% (default) to apply the classification.
scoring.result: If both thresholds are met, the document gets a POCO+ tag and your chosen classifications. If not, it gets a POCO- tag.
scoring.formula: POCO Score = (OCR × 3 + Filename × 1 + Verification) / Total Max Weight × 100%
scoring.example: Example: A rule has 4 OCR patterns (3 match), 1 filename pattern (matches), and no verification. OCR contributes 3×3=9 out of 3×4=12, filename contributes 1×1=1 out of 1×1=1. Total: (9+1)/(12+1) = 77% → passes the 75% threshold.
scoring.seeGuide: For a detailed explanation, see the

gettingStarted.title: Getting Started
gettingStarted.step1Desc: Go to Settings → Data Validation and ensure all required fields and tags exist in Paperless.
gettingStarted.step2Desc: Go to Rules and click "New Rule". The wizard will guide you through naming your rule, adding OCR patterns, and choosing what classifications to apply.
gettingStarted.step3Desc: Go to Rule Evaluation, select your rule and some documents, then click "Dry Run" to see what would happen without making changes.
gettingStarted.step4Desc: Once satisfied with Dry Run results, click "Run" to apply classifications to documents in Paperless.
gettingStarted.step5Desc: Go to Settings → Background Processing and enable automatic mode. PocoClass will now classify new documents automatically.

tutorial.pageSubtitle: Learn how to build classification rules with a real bank statement example

0_1.title: Welcome to the Rule Builder Tutorial!
0_1.text: This is where you create classification rules. Each rule teaches PocoClass how to recognize a specific type of document. Let's walk through building a rule for bank statements.

0_2.title: 6 Steps to Build a Rule
0_2.text: Every classification rule is built in 6 steps, shown in this progress bar. Each step focuses on a different aspect — from naming your rule, to defining patterns, to assigning metadata. You'll complete them left to right.

0_3.title: The Input Area
0_3.text: This is where you'll fill in your rule data. Each step shows different input fields on this side. The fields change as you move through the 6 steps.

0_4.title: Configuration Summary
0_4.text: At the bottom of each step, you'll find a configuration summary. This shows an overview of your current settings and highlights anything that might need attention with warnings.

0_5.title: PDF Preview
0_5.text: This panel shows a preview of the document you're building a rule for. Use it to identify text patterns and visual elements that are unique to this type of document.

textParts.text: This tab shows the raw text that PocoClass extracts from the document using OCR (Optical Character Recognition). This is what PocoClass actually 'reads' —
notice how the text order may differ from the visual PDF layout.
Your patterns need to match this text, not the PDF layout.

0_7.title: YAML Preview
0_7.text: The YAML tab shows your rule as structured data — this is what gets saved. Most users won't need to edit YAML directly, but it's useful for power users who want to fine-tune or copy rules.

1_0.title: Step 1: Basic Information
1_0.text: Let's start with the basics. In this first step, you give your rule a name, an ID, and a description. You also set the POCO Score threshold — the minimum confidence level needed to classify a document.

1_1.title: Rule Name & ID
1_1.text: Give your rule a clear name — like "Bank Statement". The Rule ID is generated automatically and is used internally. The description helps you remember what this rule does.

1_2.title: POCO Score Threshold
1_2.text: The POCO Score threshold decides how confident the system must be before classifying a document. The default is 75% — it means at least 75% of the weighted patterns must match. Too low = false positives, too high = missed documents.

textParts.text: This is the most important step. Here you define text patterns that uniquely identify your document type. A text pattern can be as simple as a word or phrase — like "Royal Bank of Scotland" or "Invoice Number".

For more flexibility, you can use regular expressions (regex) — a way to describe text patterns that handles variations like extra spaces or different formatting. The Pattern Helper button makes building regex easy, even without experience. For more details, see

2_1.title: PDF vs OCR — What PocoClass Sees

textParts.text: Before we define patterns, look at the difference between the PDF (how you see the document) and the OCR text (how PocoClass sees it).
between the PDF and OCR tabs on the right — notice how the text ordering is different! PocoClass works with the OCR text, not the visual layout.

as plain text — it appears at the bottom of every RBS statement. The search area is set to the last 10 lines, making the match faster and more precise. This group is optional — it boosts confidence but won't reject documents if missing.
With the help of the regex builder, this same pattern could be written as
— which would also match variations like "RoyalBank of Scotland" or "Royal   Bank of Scotland" with unusual spacing. Simple text works for most cases; regex adds flexibility when needed.
This pattern searches for your full IBAN
in the first 10 lines of the document — where bank statements typically show account details.
This group has been made mandatory

, meaning the rule will only pass if this pattern is found. This is important when you have multiple accounts with Royal Bank — it ensures you identify the correct account's statement, not just any RBS document.

You can add more logic groups to improve accuracy. Each rule needs a minimum of 3 and can have up to 10 groups. Groups can use AND logic (all conditions must match) or OR logic (any condition can match). In our example, groups 3-5 look for
The Pattern Helper button (look for the wand icon) helps you build regex patterns without needing to know regex syntax. For more details, see
The OCR Threshold (75%) sets the minimum percentage of OCR patterns that must match before the document can be classified. This ensures a base level of text-based confidence.

The Weight Multiplier (x3) controls how much OCR patterns count toward the final POCO Score. A rule can use three sources to identify documents: OCR text patterns (the main source), filename patterns, and Paperless-ngx classifications. The multiplier lets you prioritize OCR over the other sources. For more details, see

3_0.title: Step 3: Filename Patterns
3_0.text: Besides OCR text, the filename itself can help identify documents. For example, your bank might provide automated digital statements with consistent filenames like "RBS_Statement_2014-12-21.pdf". Filename patterns let you leverage this.

textParts.text: matches any filename containing "RBS" followed by any characters and then "statement". The .* means any number of characters can appear between them — matching "RBS_Statement_2014-12-21.pdf" or "RBS-Monthly-Statement.pdf".

looks for a full date pattern in the filename — four digits, a dash, two digits, a dash, and two digits. This matches the "2014-12-21" part in "RBS_Statement_2014-12-21.pdf", helping identify documents by their date stamp.

The Filename Weight Multiplier (x1) controls how much filename matches contribute to the final POCO Score — similar to the OCR multiplier you saw earlier. The default of x1 means filename patterns count less than OCR patterns (which use x3).
This makes sense because filenames are less reliable than document content — they can be renamed or inconsistent. For more details about how all multipliers work together, see

4_0.title: Step 4: Data Verification
4_0.text: Data verification adds an extra layer of confidence by cross-referencing extracted data with existing Paperless-ngx metadata. This step is optional and mainly useful for advanced setups where you want to validate document data against known values.

4_1.title: Verification Placeholders
4_1.text: Here you select which Paperless-ngx fields to use for verification. When enabled, PocoClass checks if the value assigned in Step 5 matches what's already in Paperless-ngx — adding a trust multiplier to the final score. In our example, Correspondent and Document Type are enabled for verification.

textParts.text: The verification multiplier controls how much weight Paperless-ngx field matches add to the final POCO Score. By default, the mode is set to "Auto" which neutralises the weight of this source — keeping verification as a gentle confidence boost rather than a dominant factor.

Without Auto mode, the scoring calculation could give Paperless classifications a very high weight (up to x5), potentially overshadowing your OCR patterns. Auto mode prevents this by dynamically adjusting the multiplier. For more details, see

5_0.title: Step 5: Document Classifications
5_0.text: Now that PocoClass knows how to identify your documents, it's time to tell it what to do when a match is found. In this step, you define which classification data to assign — the correspondent, document type, and tags for organization. You can also configure dynamic data extraction, which pulls specific values directly from the document text — like account numbers, invoice totals, or dates — and stores them in Paperless-ngx fields automatically.

textParts.text: These fields come directly from your Paperless-ngx instance — Correspondent, Document Type, and Tags are dropdown selections from values that already exist in Paperless-ngx. Date Created is different — it's a free date input that lets you set a specific date. In our example, we assign
as document type, and add the tags

Notice that Date Created has been left empty here on purpose. Setting a fixed date here would apply the same date to every document this rule matches — which rarely makes sense. This is where dynamic extraction really shines. Paperless-ngx has its own built-in date detection, but you have no control over which date it picks. In our bank statement, Paperless might choose 22 October 2014 instead of the correct statement end date of 21 December 2014. With dynamic extraction, you tell PocoClass exactly where to find the right date.

Custom fields let you store additional data specific to your workflow. The fields shown here depend on your Field Visibility Settings — you may see more or fewer fields depending on your configuration.

Each custom field can be set to a fixed value here in the predefined section, or extracted dynamically from the document text (configured in the next section). For more details about field configuration, see

Dynamic extraction uses anchor patterns to locate and pull specific values from the document text. Each extraction rule defines a "before anchor" (text that appears just before the value you want) and optionally an "after anchor" (text that appears just after it). The extracted text between these anchors is then stored in a Paperless-ngx field.

This is one of PocoClass's most powerful features. Combined with custom fields, you can extract virtually any information from your documents — an account number from a bank statement, the total amount from an invoice, consumed kWh from an electricity bill, or a policy number from an insurance document. Each target field can only be used once — either as a predefined value or as a dynamic extraction, never both.

5_4.title: Finding the Date in the OCR Text

textParts.text: Let's see how dynamic extraction solves the date problem from Step 5.1. Look at the OCR text — find the highlighted line
. We want to extract the end date
To build the before anchor, start with the human-readable text you can see:
. This translates into the regex pattern
— which matches any date followed by "to". The Regex Helper (wand icon) is really useful here to build these patterns step by step without needing to know regex syntax.
From the previous step, we identified the before anchor
to extract the statement end date. You can see these values filled in on the left — the target field is set to
, so "21 Dec 2014" will be written directly to the document's Date Created field in Paperless-ngx.
The after anchor is optional but recommended

— it limits the search area so PocoClass stops looking once it reaches that boundary. This helps prevent false matches when a document contains many similar values, and catches mistakes early. In this example, an after anchor is not strictly needed because the before anchor is precise enough, but for more complex documents it's good practice to constrain the search.

6_0.title: Step 6: Review & Summary
6_0.text: The final step brings everything together. Here you can review your complete rule configuration, see how the POCO scoring calculation works with your settings, and preview the generated YAML that defines your rule.

6_1.title: Rule Configuration Review
6_1.text: This overview shows all your rule settings at a glance — basic information, OCR patterns and thresholds, filename patterns, verification fields, and document classifications. Use it to double-check everything before saving.

6_2.title: POCO Score Calculation
6_2.text: This section shows exactly how the POCO Score is calculated for your rule. It breaks down the maximum possible weights from each source (OCR, filename, verification), shows an example calculation at 80% match rate, and displays both score thresholds that must be met for classification.

6_3.title: That's It!
6_3.text: On the right you can see the generated YAML — the actual rule file that PocoClass uses. You've just seen how a rule is built from start to finish. The key idea: find patterns that uniquely identify your document type, set sensible thresholds, and let PocoClass do the rest. Start simple with a few mandatory OCR patterns — you can always refine later.
